name: Tefnut
description: Tefnut is the ancient Egyptian goddess of moisture, water, humidity, and dew. She is a daughter of Ra, the sun god, created either through his spitting or sneezing, depending on the account. Alongside her brother and consort, Shu, the god of air, she forms one of the first divine couples in the Ennead of Heliopolis, representing fundamental aspects of the cosmos. Tefnut's presence is vital for maintaining the balance of the world, as moisture is essential for life and fertility. She is often depicted as a lioness-headed woman, symbolizing her fierce and protective nature. In some myths, she becomes angered with Ra and flees to Nubia, withholding her life-giving moisture from Egypt, causing drought and hardship. Thoth and Shu eventually persuade her to return, restoring balance and prosperity to the land. Her return is celebrated as a symbol of renewal and the cyclical nature of life. Tefnut embodies the power of water to both nurture and destroy, and her role in the creation myth highlights the importance of moisture in the Egyptian worldview. She is crucial for the continuation of life and the prosperity of the land.
subtitle: Goddess of Moisture, Dew, and Rain

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Tefnut
keyMyths.description: Tefnut was created by Ra, the sun god, through either spitting or sneezing. This event marked the beginning of the separation of the cosmos and the emergence of fundamental elements.

keyMyths.title: Tefnut's Flight to Nubia
keyMyths.description: Angered with Ra, Tefnut fled to Nubia, causing a severe drought in Egypt. Shu and Thoth were sent to convince her to return, restoring moisture and fertility to the land.

keyMyths.title: The Return of Tefnut
keyMyths.description: Tefnut's return from Nubia was celebrated as a joyous occasion, symbolizing renewal and the restoration of cosmic balance. Her presence brought back the life-giving waters and ensured the prosperity of Egypt.

sources.author: Richard H. Wilkinson
sources.work: Complete Gods and Goddesses of Ancient Egypt

sources.author: Erik Hornung
sources.work: Conceptions of God in Ancient Egypt
sources.description: Exploration of the theological concepts surrounding Egyptian deities.
sources.text: Exploration of the theological concepts surrounding Egyptian deities.
